item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great job, very creative. Only nitpick would be that some sentences could be more formal, but that's it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very minor nitpick:

"Several machining tools were found in the basement where the device was kept…the source of the anomaly as a house in Orlando, Florida."

It's vanishingly rare to have a house with a basement in Orlando, FL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It may be very slightly little heavy-handed with the explanation, but it put a dumb smile on my face throughout and cemented it at the end. (Kinda feels like positivity is a thread in the contest so far?) +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, why does it start by saying physical containment is irrelevant? I've read plenty SCPs in which it was also irrelevant, but it was never quite said that way.

I can't really speak to the other part of this question, but regarding this: This was primarily a meta tactic to set my eventual co-author up to be free. My idea for the Con Procs was CRITICAL CURTAIN, which was a simple but detailed concept. However, I realized what I had written didn't cover the SCP itself at all, so I tossed out a single, short sentence to touch on that, one that would hopefully be vague enough my partner could do a lot with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why would it build a nuke, there's plenty already — and why preemptively kill cephalopods when you can just nuke the real deal when/if it shows up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good, but a couple minor things. First, why does it start by saying physical containment is irrelevant? I've read plenty SCPs in which it was also irrelevant, but it was never quite said that way. Second, why did they stop the interview? They have amnestics and they've used them before in similar situations, isn't the interview important enough, especially since it seems the entity is indeed answering their questions?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always figured there's a handful of anomalies that haven't been contained by the Foundation by the virtue of how minor they are.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked how it came back around to why the containment procedures are as is. I do also think that this is a more realistic way the Foundation would engage a low threat entity, instead of exterminating/ emprisoning it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it works because if humans can beat Cthulhu, then "P" does not need to worry about Cthulhu and it can just make watermelons taste bad again.

item.title: Re: Author Post II
item.content:encoded:
I'm the author of the ConProcs here! I'm very glad to see how this turned out; I tried to set up the procs in a way that left a lot of room to jump off from.

Credit to Waxwing-one for the concept of the phylum Eldritch from SCP-4501.

And thank you to Uncle Nicolini, Lt Flops and Oboebandgeek99 for crit on the ConProcs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That’s fair. I was trying to avoid using the word “memetic”, since it’s one of those words that I feel has become overused on this site. I also could’ve probably picked a better idea to flow with the containment procedures (although I didn’t really want to write that much about eldritch entities).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This didn’t work for me. The description didn’t present the containment subject in a way that I could understand. (For example, what does it mean for a “concept” to be sapient? Isn’t there a better way of explaining this, like “incorporeal and non-localized sapient entity”?)

And to my mind, the body of the article didn’t flow organically from the containment procedures you were given, which seems like coloring outside the lines of the spirit of the contest. You’re trying to hammer a square peg into a round hole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You’re right. I don’t know how that slipped through my proofreading process.

Normally this would be a big enough issue that I would delete this SCP and reupload it at a later date, once everything is fixed. However, that feels unfair to my co-author. So I’m just going to say "it works because it works", and see how far that takes us.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was some over-the-top conprocs you received, but you have managed to do some justice. It's a pretty interesting anomaly that works well with the conprocs, and you also shifted the center of the article to something larger, as well as presented an article with steady escalation. Well deserved +1 for the spirit of the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, that bothered me too. And it's the only containment measure the foundation seems to have taken against -5054, which seems at the very least dangerous, for the reasons Niblic mentioned above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is certainly an enjoyable read, but i have a plot related question:

how is CRITICAL CURTAIN an effective containment measure against SCP-5054 in any way?

if we convince it that "hey, cthulhu can be harmed by nukes", wouldn't its first priority be to build a nuke and mass murder a bunch of octopuses?

i apologize if i'm misunderstanding something, but this stuck out as a big enough plot hole that i'm inclined to novote for now.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Corpsecon, baby!

p.jpg is my own original creation, device.jpg is https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pusher_Centrifuge.jpg

Thank you to DianaBerry, Eliminator_V5, Mew-ltiverse, KaraKatt, Dr Moned, Veralta, PlaguePJP, r0se and Ferno86 for crits.
